title: Jamaican oxtail stew
subtitle: Oxtail slow-braised with allspice, Scotch bonnet, and butter beans until gelatinous and rich
excerpt: Oxtail pieces marinated in allspice, thyme, and dark soy, then slow-braised for three to four hours until the collagen has melted and the meat is falling from the bone in a thick, deeply savoury sauce. Finished with butter beans and spring onions. One of the great braises of Caribbean cooking.
sourceNotes: Jamaican oxtail stew is documented in Caribbean cookery literature from the nineteenth century. The use of oxtail (a cheap cut prized for its high collagen content) reflects the history of Caribbean cooking, in which enslaved people made use of the cuts left after the prized parts were taken. The dish is a cornerstone of the Jamaican and wider Caribbean kitchen. Multiple cross-referenced Caribbean culinary sources.

content.text: Oxtail is almost entirely collagen. After three hours of gentle braising, that collagen melts into the sauce and produces a liquid that is so thick it sets to a soft jelly when cooled. This is the quality you are cooking towards: a sauce that coats the meat heavily and is viscous rather than watery. You cannot rush it. Two hours of braising produces a good result; three hours produces the right one.

Ask the butcher to cut the oxtail into 4 to 5 cm sections. The pieces vary in size along the tail, the large sections from the thick end are meatier, the small sections from the narrow end add mainly gelatine. Both are useful. The kidney beans can be replaced with butter beans, which are more traditional in Jamaica.

Season the {{oxtail}} of oxtail pieces with the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, {{black-pepper}} of pepper, {{allspice}} of allspice, and {{thyme-dried}} of dried thyme. Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of oil in a dutch oven over high heat and brown the oxtail in batches for 4 to 5 minutes per side until very deeply coloured. Remove and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} onions to the pot and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{soy-sauce-dark}} of dark soy sauce and stir. Return the oxtail to the pot. Pour in the {{stock-beef}} of stock. Add the {{scotch-bonnet}} Scotch bonnet whole. Bring to a simmer, cover, and transfer to the oven at 160°C (fan 140°C). Braise for 2 hours 30 minutes, checking once to ensure the liquid has not dried out.

Add the {{kidney-beans}} of kidney beans and {{spring-onion}} of spring onions, stir gently, and return to the oven uncovered for 30 minutes until the sauce is thick and glossy. Remove the Scotch bonnet. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve over white rice.

items.fix: Cover and return to the oven for another 30 to 60 minutes; the meat should be so tender it falls from the bone with no pressure
items.cause: Not enough time; oxtail needs a minimum of 2.5 hours
items.symptom: Meat is tough and not pulling from the bone

items.fix: Remove the lid and cook at 180°C for 20 to 30 minutes to reduce and concentrate; the collagen in the oxtail thickens the sauce naturally over time
items.cause: Too much stock, or not cooked uncovered at the end
items.symptom: Sauce is thin and not glossy

items.fix: When using dark soy, reduce the added salt by half; taste before seasoning at each stage
items.cause: Dark soy sauce adds significant salt on top of the seasoning

content.text: Oxtail stew is one of the defining dishes of the Caribbean kitchen, and its history is bound to the history of slavery. The cuts given to enslaved people on Jamaican plantations were the parts the plantation owners did not want, tails, tripe, feet, offal. The cooking that developed from these ingredients was inventive, patient, and deeply flavoured. Oxtail in particular became a prestige dish as time passed, expensive and time-consuming to cook well. The Scotch bonnet and allspice are native to or closely associated with Jamaica; the dark, thick braising sauce is the same technique applied to whatever was at hand.
